items.passage:
The proscenium arch is a framing structure in theater architecture that separates the stage from the seating area, presenting each performance as if viewed through a large window. It emerged in European playhouses during the seventeenth century, when increasingly elaborate painted scenery was displayed. The arch marks a clear boundary, known as the picture frame, between the imagined world of the drama and the real space of the audience.

This framing device works chiefly by shaping how audiences perceive space. [■] The arch hides the side wings and the overhead rigging, so painted scenery appears to hang within a single bordered picture. [■] Because every spectator looks through the same opening, designers can calibrate one ideal viewpoint for the entire house, and objects placed near the frame seem larger than those set deep upstage.

These illusions gave the proscenium its lasting influence on design. [■] Opera houses and grand theaters often adopted the frame to stage painterly, large-scale spectacles for seated crowds. [■] Architects now apply the same principles when they design cinema screens and exhibition halls, using a bordered opening to focus attention and to hide the mechanics behind a scene.
paragraphs: The proscenium arch is a framing structure in theater architecture that separates the stage from the seating area, presenting each performance as if viewed through a large window. It emerged in European playhouses during the seventeenth century, when increasingly elaborate painted scenery was displayed. The arch marks a clear boundary, known as the picture frame, between the imagined world of the drama and the real space of the audience.

This framing device works chiefly by shaping how audiences perceive space. [■] The arch hides the side wings and the overhead rigging, so painted scenery appears to hang within a single bordered picture. [■] Because every spectator looks through the same opening, designers can calibrate one ideal viewpoint for the entire house, and objects placed near the frame seem larger than those set deep upstage.

These illusions gave the proscenium its lasting influence on design. [■] Opera houses and grand theaters often adopted the frame to stage painterly, large-scale spectacles for seated crowds. [■] Architects now apply the same principles when they design cinema screens and exhibition halls, using a bordered opening to focus attention and to hide the mechanics behind a scene.

questions.stem: The word "calibrate" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: enlarge
options.B: adjust
options.C: conceal
options.D: imagine

questions.explanation: The passage says designers "calibrate one ideal viewpoint for the entire house," meaning they set or adjust it precisely, so "adjust" fits the context.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does the arch make painted scenery appear to hang within a single bordered picture?

options.A: By widening the opening so more of the stage is visible.
options.B: By tilting the stage floor toward the audience.
options.C: By concealing the side wings and the rigging above the stage.
options.D: By dimming the auditorium during the performance.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "the arch hides the side wings and the overhead rigging, so painted scenery appears to hang within a single bordered picture."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about spectators seated at the edges of the auditorium?

options.A: They enjoy the clearest possible view of the painted scenery.
options.B: They sit nearest to the concealed stage machinery.
options.C: They lose sight of the performance entirely.
options.D: They perceive the intended illusion less perfectly than central viewers.

questions.explanation: Because designers "calibrate one ideal viewpoint for the entire house," viewers away from that single vantage point would experience the illusion less perfectly.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention opera houses and grand theaters in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that theaters were more advanced than early cinemas.
options.B: To show that the frame actually limited scenic display.
options.C: To give examples of institutions that adopted the frame for spectacle.
options.D: To describe in detail how one opera house was constructed.

questions.explanation: The sentence notes that "opera houses and grand theaters often adopted the frame to stage painterly, large-scale spectacles," illustrating the arch's lasting influence.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, painters covered the backdrops with rows of receding columns, tricking the eye into perceiving depth where the stage was in fact shallow.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location
options.B: The second marked location
options.C: The third marked location
options.D: The fourth marked location

questions.explanation: The sentence is a concrete example of "shaping how audiences perceive space," so it fits best right after that topic sentence at the first marker.

items.passage:
A comet is a small icy body that orbits the Sun and that, when heated, may release gas and dust to form a glowing head and a long tail. Most comets spend the greater part of their orbits in the cold outer Solar System, far from the Sun's warmth. Only when one approaches the inner planets does it brighten enough to be seen from Earth.

This visible activity originates in the comet's nucleus, a solid core of ice and dust only a few kilometers across. [■] As the body nears the Sun, its surface ices absorb heat and pass directly from solid to gas, a process known as sublimation. [■] The escaping gas then drags fine dust outward, so that a coma and one or more tails stream away from the tiny core.

Modern studies have narrowed these broad ideas into detailed measurements. [■] Spacecraft that flew past Halley's Comet sampled its gas directly, and water was confirmed as the dominant ice. [■] Individual molecules within the coma are now identified from the ground, and continued observation promises even more precise portraits of these ancient travelers.
paragraphs: A comet is a small icy body that orbits the Sun and that, when heated, may release gas and dust to form a glowing head and a long tail. Most comets spend the greater part of their orbits in the cold outer Solar System, far from the Sun's warmth. Only when one approaches the inner planets does it brighten enough to be seen from Earth.

This visible activity originates in the comet's nucleus, a solid core of ice and dust only a few kilometers across. [■] As the body nears the Sun, its surface ices absorb heat and pass directly from solid to gas, a process known as sublimation. [■] The escaping gas then drags fine dust outward, so that a coma and one or more tails stream away from the tiny core.

Modern studies have narrowed these broad ideas into detailed measurements. [■] Spacecraft that flew past Halley's Comet sampled its gas directly, and water was confirmed as the dominant ice. [■] Individual molecules within the coma are now identified from the ground, and continued observation promises even more precise portraits of these ancient travelers.

questions.stem: The word "dominant" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: principal
options.B: frozen
options.C: hidden
options.D: heaviest

questions.explanation: Spacecraft "confirmed" water "as the dominant ice," meaning the main or principal component, so "principal" fits.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens to the comet's surface ices as it nears the Sun?

options.A: They melt into liquid before slowly evaporating.
options.B: They freeze more solidly on the shaded side.
options.C: They pass directly from solid to gas.
options.D: They sink toward the center of the nucleus.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the "surface ices absorb heat and pass directly from solid to gas, a process known as sublimation."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: A comet's nucleus measures only a few kilometers across.
options.B: A comet's tail trails directly behind it along its orbit.
options.C: Escaping gas carries fine dust outward to form the coma.
options.D: Spacecraft found water to be the comet's dominant ice.

questions.explanation: The passage never states that the tail trails along the orbit; it says only that a coma and tails "stream away from the tiny core." The other three are stated directly.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about earlier understanding of comet composition?

options.A: Earlier scientists had already identified every molecule.
options.B: Early studies relied mainly on meteorite samples.
options.C: Early ideas were entirely mistaken about ice.
options.D: Earlier understanding was broader and less precise than today's.

questions.explanation: Because "modern studies have narrowed these broad ideas into detailed measurements," earlier knowledge must have been more general and less exact.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a nucleus no larger than a small town can, when warmed, generate a tail stretching millions of kilometers across the sky.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location
options.B: The second marked location
options.C: The third marked location
options.D: The fourth marked location

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates the surprising contrast of the tiny nucleus "only a few kilometers across" producing a vast tail, so it fits best at the first marker.

items.passage:
Dead reckoning is a navigational method that estimates a ship's present position by applying its measured speed, its steered heading, and the time elapsed to a position already known. It served for centuries as the only means sailors had to cross open water out of sight of land. The technique demanded no view of the stars, only careful and continuous record-keeping.

The method depends on three measured quantities combined in sequence. [■] Sailors gauged speed with a log line, a knotted rope streamed behind the hull and timed against a sandglass. [■] They read direction from a magnetic compass and marked elapsed time by the turning of the same glass. [■] From these figures a navigator advanced the ship's estimated place across the chart, hour by hour.

These running estimates made long ocean crossings possible well before accurate clocks existed. [■] Because each reading carried a small error, mistakes accumulated over days, and the reckoned position was pushed away from the true one. Researchers are still reconstructing surviving logbooks to measure how far these drifts often carried early voyages off course.
paragraphs: Dead reckoning is a navigational method that estimates a ship's present position by applying its measured speed, its steered heading, and the time elapsed to a position already known. It served for centuries as the only means sailors had to cross open water out of sight of land. The technique demanded no view of the stars, only careful and continuous record-keeping.

The method depends on three measured quantities combined in sequence. [■] Sailors gauged speed with a log line, a knotted rope streamed behind the hull and timed against a sandglass. [■] They read direction from a magnetic compass and marked elapsed time by the turning of the same glass. [■] From these figures a navigator advanced the ship's estimated place across the chart, hour by hour.

These running estimates made long ocean crossings possible well before accurate clocks existed. [■] Because each reading carried a small error, mistakes accumulated over days, and the reckoned position was pushed away from the true one. Researchers are still reconstructing surviving logbooks to measure how far these drifts often carried early voyages off course.

questions.stem: The word "gauged" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: increased
options.B: recorded
options.C: measured
options.D: balanced

questions.explanation: Sailors "gauged speed with a log line," that is, they measured it, so "measured" fits the context.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how did sailors determine the ship's direction?

options.A: By timing a knotted rope against a sandglass.
options.B: By observing the changing position of the Sun.
options.C: By following the flow of the ocean currents.
options.D: By reading a magnetic compass.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that sailors "read direction from a magnetic compass."

questions.stem: What does "the same glass" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the magnetic compass
options.B: the knotted rope
options.C: the log line
options.D: the sandglass

questions.explanation: Speed was "timed against a sandglass," and elapsed time was marked "by the turning of the same glass," so the phrase refers back to the sandglass.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about long ocean voyages guided by dead reckoning?

options.A: Longer voyages tended to end with larger positional errors.
options.B: Errors in position shrank as a voyage continued.
options.C: Navigators abandoned the method on very long crossings.
options.D: The method proved entirely useless without accurate mechanical clocks.

questions.explanation: Because "mistakes accumulated over days" and pushed the reckoned position away from the true one, more days at sea would produce greater error.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, counting how many knots slipped away in half a minute gave sailors a direct reading of speed.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: The first marked location
options.B: The second marked location
options.C: The third marked location
options.D: The fourth marked location

questions.explanation: The sentence explains how the log line's knots yielded a speed reading, so it fits best just after the sentence describing the log line at the second marker.

items.passage:
Sleep inertia is a temporary state of reduced alertness and impaired performance that occurs immediately after a person wakes from sleep. During this period, reaction times lengthen and decisions become slower and less accurate. The feeling is familiar as the heavy grogginess that can cloud the first minutes of the day.

This grogginess arises because the brain does not switch on all at once. Regions that support basic arousal wake quickly; the prefrontal cortex, which governs planning and judgment, reactivates more slowly. Measurements of cerebral blood flow show that this lag can last from a few minutes to nearly an hour. Waking from deep slow-wave sleep tends to produce the strongest and longest effects.

This slow return to full function matters wherever sharp decisions must follow abrupt waking, as for emergency responders and on-call medical staff. The effect is often reduced by short naps that stay clear of deep sleep. However, laboratory findings face objections, because staged awakenings in a quiet lab rarely match the disorder of real overnight shift work.
paragraphs: Sleep inertia is a temporary state of reduced alertness and impaired performance that occurs immediately after a person wakes from sleep. During this period, reaction times lengthen and decisions become slower and less accurate. The feeling is familiar as the heavy grogginess that can cloud the first minutes of the day.

This grogginess arises because the brain does not switch on all at once. Regions that support basic arousal wake quickly; the prefrontal cortex, which governs planning and judgment, reactivates more slowly. Measurements of cerebral blood flow show that this lag can last from a few minutes to nearly an hour. Waking from deep slow-wave sleep tends to produce the strongest and longest effects.

This slow return to full function matters wherever sharp decisions must follow abrupt waking, as for emergency responders and on-call medical staff. The effect is often reduced by short naps that stay clear of deep sleep. However, laboratory findings face objections, because staged awakenings in a quiet lab rarely match the disorder of real overnight shift work.

questions.stem: The word "abrupt" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: sudden
options.B: gradual
options.C: planned
options.D: delayed

questions.explanation: The passage refers to "sharp decisions" that "follow abrupt waking," that is, sudden waking, so "sudden" fits.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, which brain region reactivates more slowly after waking?

options.A: The regions that govern basic arousal.
options.B: The visual cortex at the back of the brain.
options.C: The brainstem beneath the cortex.
options.D: The prefrontal cortex.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "the prefrontal cortex, which governs planning and judgment, reactivates more slowly."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about waking from deep slow-wave sleep?

options.A: It leaves a person feeling immediately alert.
options.B: It occurs mainly in the early hours of morning.
options.C: Tasks requiring judgment are harder right after such waking.
options.D: It stops the prefrontal cortex from ever functioning.

questions.explanation: Since deep slow-wave sleep "tends to produce the strongest and longest effects," the slowed prefrontal cortex would make judgment-heavy tasks harder just afterward.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention emergency responders and on-call medical staff in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that these jobs cause long-term chronic insomnia.
options.B: To show that sleep inertia rarely matters in practice.
options.C: To give examples of settings where the effect can be dangerous.
options.D: To describe the daily schedule of a single night nurse.

questions.explanation: These workers are named as people for whom "sharp decisions must follow abrupt waking," illustrating where sleep inertia carries real risk.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Sleep inertia reduces alertness right after waking.
options.B: Sleep inertia persists for several hours after waking.
options.C: The prefrontal cortex reactivates comparatively slowly.
options.D: Brief naps that avoid deep sleep can reduce the effect.

questions.explanation: The passage says the lag lasts "from a few minutes to nearly an hour," not several hours; the other statements are all supported.

items.passage:
Food storage in early agricultural societies refers to the techniques farmers developed to preserve harvested grain for use long after the growing season ended. Early farmers faced a basic problem: harvests arrived in a brief burst, yet families needed food throughout the whole year. Damp, insects, and decay threatened any grain that was left unprotected.

These pressures drove the invention of dedicated storage structures. Communities dug sealed underground pits and raised granaries on wooden posts to keep grain dry and away from rodents. Some groups also parched the seed before storing it, killing the sprouts and insects that would otherwise ruin the supply.

Reliable stores reshaped how these societies lived. A dependable surplus let populations settle permanently and freed some members from the daily search for food. That wealth was guarded and administered, though, which demanded new organization, and control over the granaries often grew into an early source of inequality. This connection ties the practical problem of keeping grain dry to the emergence of the first social hierarchies.
paragraphs: Food storage in early agricultural societies refers to the techniques farmers developed to preserve harvested grain for use long after the growing season ended. Early farmers faced a basic problem: harvests arrived in a brief burst, yet families needed food throughout the whole year. Damp, insects, and decay threatened any grain that was left unprotected.

These pressures drove the invention of dedicated storage structures. Communities dug sealed underground pits and raised granaries on wooden posts to keep grain dry and away from rodents. Some groups also parched the seed before storing it, killing the sprouts and insects that would otherwise ruin the supply.

Reliable stores reshaped how these societies lived. A dependable surplus let populations settle permanently and freed some members from the daily search for food. That wealth was guarded and administered, though, which demanded new organization, and control over the granaries often grew into an early source of inequality. This connection ties the practical problem of keeping grain dry to the emergence of the first social hierarchies.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The construction of sealed underground grain-storage pits.
options.B: The specific pests and molds that damaged unprotected stored grain.
options.C: How early farmers stored harvests and how storage reshaped society.
options.D: The reasons early farmers gradually abandoned permanent settled life.

questions.explanation: The passage moves from the storage problem to its structural solutions and finally to the social changes that followed, tying "keeping grain dry to the emergence of the first social hierarchies."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did some groups do to the seed before storing it?

options.A: They parched the seed to kill sprouts and insects.
options.B: They buried the seed inside raised granaries on posts.
options.C: They soaked the seed in heavily salted water.
options.D: They ground the seed into flour immediately.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "some groups also parched the seed before storing it, killing the sprouts and insects that would otherwise ruin the supply."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: Paragraph 2 questions the claims made in paragraph 1.
options.B: Paragraph 2 gives distant historical background for paragraph 1.
options.C: Paragraph 2 simply restates the problem described in paragraph 1.
options.D: Paragraph 2 describes solutions to the problem raised in paragraph 1.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 sets out the storage problem, and paragraph 2 presents the "dedicated storage structures" invented in response, so it supplies the solution.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about communities that held large grain surpluses?

options.A: They are likely to have remained equal in wealth and status.
options.B: They probably developed leaders who controlled the stored food.
options.C: They traded most surplus grain with distant regions.
options.D: They abandoned farming once large surpluses appeared.

questions.explanation: Because "control over the granaries often grew into an early source of inequality," some individuals likely gained power by managing the stored surplus.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what problem did early farmers face?

options.A: Grain sprouted too quickly inside underground pits.
options.B: Harvests arrived briefly, yet food was needed year-round.
options.C: Farmers could not find enough seed to plant.
options.D: Wild animals destroyed the growing crops in the field.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states the problem directly: "harvests arrived in a brief burst, yet families needed food throughout the whole year."
